It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. Gulbrandsen Fridtjof Nansen Institute, Lysaker, Norway Abstract Research has recognized that states enable or constrain private governance initiatives, but we still know too little about the interactions between private and public authority in the governance of various social and environmental problems. This article examines how states have responded to the emergence of forest and ﬁsheries certiﬁcation programs, and how state responses have inﬂuenced the subsequent development of these programs. It is argued that historical and structural differences in the management of forest and ﬁsheries have resulted in divergent state responses to certiﬁcation programs, but that both trajectories of interaction have led to a strengthening of the non-state program. The article draws upon these cases to inductively identify types of interaction between state policies and non-state certiﬁcation programs, the causal mechanisms that shed light on interaction dynamics, and the conditions under which state involve- ment is likely to result in either strengthening or weakening of non-state programs. Keywords: co-regulation, ﬁsheries certiﬁcation, forest certiﬁcation, governance interactions, private authority. 1. Introduction Non-state certiﬁcation programs have emerged as a vibrant source of “transnational business governance” (Eberlein et al. 2014) across policy sectors and industries, including forestry, ﬁsh- eries, aquaculture, organic agriculture, coffee production, palm oil production, and parks man- agement. These programs typically establish environmental performance standards and standards for socially responsible production. They go beyond voluntary codes of conduct and self-regulatory modes of governing, by requiring independent veriﬁcation of compliance with standards. They also constitute governing arenas in which a wide range of stakeholders interact and agree upon rules and governance mechanisms (Bernstein & Cashore 2007). Scholars have recognized that governments enable or constrain private governance initia- tives, but the dynamic interactions between private and public authority in the governance of various social and environmental problems have remained an understudied area of contempo- rary global governance (Vogel 2010; Eberlein et al. 2014). This article examines public–private governance interactions through a comparison of forest and ﬁsheries certiﬁcation programs. These programs are interesting to study because they arguably represent the most advanced cases of non-state governance dynamics in global environmental politics (Cashore 2002; Gulbrandsen Correspondence: Lars H. They are interesting to compare because of differences in the management of forests and of ﬁsheries (Gulbrandsen 2010). Forests are national resources managed by domestic authorities, companies with logging concessions, and forest owners. In contrast, marine ﬁsh stocks are common pool resources managed by governments through international, regional, and domestic management regimes. While efforts to establish a binding global forest convention have met with little success, there is a long history of international governance in the ﬁsheries sector and a wide range of legally binding rules, centered in recent decades on the 1982 Law of the Sea Convention.1 This article argues that historical and structural differences in the management of forests and ﬁsheries have led to different state responses to certiﬁcation programs in the two sectors, but that both trajectories of interaction have resulted in a strengthening of the non-state programs. I draw upon these cases in inductively identifying pathways of interaction between state-based regulation and non-state certiﬁcation programs and the causal mechanisms that shed light on the dynamics of interaction. I also identify conditions under which state involvement is likely to result in either strengthening or weakening of non-state programs. Future research should examine whether these conditions are speciﬁc to the forestry and ﬁsheries cases or are applicable to other cases as well. The purpose of this inductive paired comparison is to identify mechanisms of governance interactions and generate hypotheses that can be examined for a larger number of cases in future research. This article proceeds as follows: Section 2 reviews work on the inﬂuence of states on non-state certiﬁcation programs and explains the exploratory, inductive approach adopted in this study. Section 3 examines state responses to forest and ﬁsheries certiﬁcation programs, and section 4 analyzes the evolutionary effects of these responses. Based on the investigation of the forestry and ﬁsheries cases, section 5 identiﬁes processes, mechanisms, and hypothesized effects of interaction that can guide future research. Section 6 concludes brieﬂy. 2. The role of states in non-state certiﬁcation programs There is now a range of voluntary standards and programs across issue areas, supplementing and sometimes supplanting traditional government regulations (see e.g. Cashore 2002; Bartley 2007; Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Potoski & Prakash 2009). While many voluntary environmental programs have been sponsored by state regulators (Coglianese & Nash 2009; Prakash & Potoski 2012), the focus of this article is on certiﬁcation programs that have been created with little or no involvement from governments. Indeed, in a much-cited volume on “private authority” in ﬁelds from technical standards to credit rating, Cutler et al. (1999, p. 16) maintain that non-state actors are “increasingly engaged in authoritative decision-making that was previously the pre- rogative of sovereign states.” As traditional boycott campaigns often failed to encourage compa- nies to opt for more sustainable production practices, the creation of non-state certiﬁcation programs was, in many cases, a non-governmental organization (NGO) effort aimed at ﬁnding new ways to inﬂuence corporate conduct. Other certiﬁcation programs emerged as a result of the efforts of industry and business to compete with or complement NGO-sponsored schemes (Cashore et al. 2004; Gulbrandsen 2010). The main claim of much of the literature on private authority is not that states do not contribute to the governance processes, but that private regulatory programs do not derive rulemaking authority from governments (Cashore 2002; Bartley 2007). Although research has recognized that states may inﬂuence private governance efforts in several ways, we still know too little about the dynamic interactions between private and public Dynamic governance interactions L. H. Gulbrandsen © 2012 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 75 Page 3: authority in the governance of various social and environmental problems. How can states inﬂuence non-state programs? It is useful to differentiate among the regulatory contributions that actor groups make at the different stages of the regulatory process, depending on their “competencies” (Abbott & Snidal 2009).2 Abbott and Snidal (2009) identify ﬁve stages of the regulatory process: agenda-setting, negotiation of standards, implementation, monitoring, and enforcement. For this analysis, I reduce these ﬁve stages to three – agenda-setting/negotiation, implementation, and monitoring/enforcement – and identify the regulatory contributions states can make at each stage. Beginning with agenda-setting and negotiation, governments can participate in these pro- cesses by providing expertise and technical advice, as well administrative or ﬁnancial support (Boström 2003; Meidinger 2006; Tollefson et al. 2008; Lister 2011). Government may inﬂuence agenda-setting and negotiation both through direct regulation and “the shadow of hierarchy.” After the 1984 Bhopal disaster in India, for instance, there has been strong legal incentive for ﬁrms in the chemical industry to join voluntary programs; this can demonstrate their due diligence and shield them from punitive ﬁnes in the event of an industrial accident. The Responsible Care program of the chemical industry emerged and diffused as a direct result of this sort of pressure (Prakash 2000). Governments can also limit the rulemaking authority of speciﬁc certiﬁcation programs by supporting the creation of competing programs or enacting regulations that restrict their discretion (Gulbrandsen 2010; Gale & Haward 2011). Similarly, existing rules and regulations can facilitate or constrain non-state standard-setting processes. The key distinction is whether governments use their competencies to restrict or enhance the rulemaking authority of non-state certiﬁcation initiatives (Cashore et al. 2004, p. 21). Concerning implementation,Abbott and Snidal (2009,p.66) argue that while the importance of states is relatively high at the agenda-setting/negotiation stage, it is relatively low at the implementation stage, as “even strong states lack the authority and expertise to implement standards within ﬁrms.”But governments can, and often do, act as clients for non-state programs through the certiﬁcation of state-controlled or -owned operations (Auld et al.2008; Gulbrandsen 2010). Indeed, by certifying such operations, states indirectly take on responsibility for imple- mentation. Governments can also require state-owned companies to adopt standards to help streamline the collection and interpretation of data on social and environmental performance, and they can provide ﬁnancial support or other resources to companies or groups seeking certiﬁcation (Boström 2003; Tollefson et al. 2008; Lister 2011). In addition to such supply-chain support,governments can be central to the uptake of certiﬁcation on the demand side (Auld et al. 2008).The most obvious type of government demand-side support is found with public procure- ment policies that stipulate the purchase of certiﬁed products. This form of support applies more readily to some resources than others: for example, procurement policies are likely to play a signiﬁcant role in the forestry sector, but not the ﬁsheries sector, because the state buys many products from the former sector, but not the latter. Whereas governments could facilitate market acceptance of certiﬁcation, they could also impede the uptake of certiﬁcation by rejecting particular schemes or labels.If governments favor one certiﬁcation scheme over another in public procurement policies,it would be a strong signal to groups considering various options.Again the key question is whether governments use their competencies to approve or reject particular certiﬁcation schemes. Finally, states may play a key role in the monitoring and enforcement of standards (Abbott & Snidal 2009, p. 69). In the natural resource programs discussed here, states are not directly involved in the monitoring and enforcement of standards. These programs rely on regular third-party audits by independent certiﬁcation bodies (certiﬁers) for enforcement. All the same, Dynamic governance interactions L. H. Gulbrandsen © 2012 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 76 Page 4: they require effective regulation and enforcement of contract law, property rights, planning rules, and the like, in order to function (Meidinger 2006), and governments can have signiﬁcant impact on the work of third-party auditors. Fisheries management auditors depend upon reliable ﬁsh-stocks data from public authorities for compliance assessments. They also depend upon effective government enforcement of ﬁsheries regulations, and sometimes upon expert advice from public agencies. Forest management auditors are less dependent on public authori- ties for assessments, but they cannot award a certiﬁcate unless there is public enforcement of forestry laws and land tenure rights. In sum, states can facilitate or impede non-state certiﬁcation at different stages of the regulatory process, as summarized in Table 1. This table does not provide an exhaustive overview of the regulatory contributions of governments, but the actions described here are believed to be the most important ones for the cases analyzed in this study. Given that little is known about the processes, outcomes, and underlying mechanisms of public–private interaction dynamics, an exploratory, inductive approach seemed the appropriate research design for this study. I proceed in three analytical steps to investigate interaction dynamics. First, I examine how states have responded to the emergence of certiﬁcation in the forest and ﬁsheries sectors. A useful indicator of state engagement is incorporation into state policies. In recent years, several countries have developed timber procurement policies and recognized certiﬁcation under credible schemes as assurance of legally and sustainably sourced timber. Hence, for the forestry case, I investigate policy documents, ofﬁcial assessments, and documentation from each country with timber procurement policies. As noted, investigating procurement policies is less relevant in the case of ﬁsheries. Here I focus instead on incorpora- tion of certiﬁcation into seafood policy strategies and how states have otherwise acted to enhance or restrict the rulemaking authority of certiﬁcation programs in the ﬁsheries sector. Second, I examine the evolutionary effects of state responses to certiﬁcation programs by investigating program uptake and the development of certiﬁcation standards, rules, and proce- dures. I draw on policy documents from forest and ﬁsheries certiﬁcation programs and primary research on these programs conducted over several years, including 27 semi-structured inter- views with representatives of the programs, environmental NGOs, industry associations, and government agencies. The interviews were not conducted speciﬁcally for the purpose of inves- tigating governance interactions, but this emerged as a key topic in many interviews. Finally, from the empirical examination of the forestry and ﬁsheries cases, I identify types and pathways of public–private governance interactions. State responses to the emergence of certiﬁcation 3.1. Forest certiﬁcation Forest certiﬁcation emerged in response to growing concern about global forest degradation and failed intergovernmental efforts to develop a legally binding agreement on forests. In 1993, the World Wildlife Fund (WWF) ofﬁcially founded the Forest Stewardship Council (FSC) in part- nership with other NGOs and social groups, as well as some retailers, forest companies, and professional certiﬁers. Backed by governments in many producer countries, forest industry and landowner associations responded to the emergence of FSC by creating “industry-friendly” certiﬁcation programs with more ﬂexible and discretionary standards. These national-level programs eventually formed an umbrella organization, since 2003 known as the Programme for the Endorsement of Forest Certiﬁcation (PEFC). Scholars have shown that governments facili- tated the development of PEFC or FSC certiﬁcation in many jurisdictions (see Boström 2003; Tollefson et al. 2008; Gale & Haward 2011; Lister 2011), but less attention has been paid to the development and effects of public procurement policies. This section provides a systematic examination of the development of such policies. The European Parliament has, on several occasions, called for European Union (EU) restric- tions on the import of tropical timber, but such restrictions have never been enacted for fear of violating multilateral trade rules. However, the EU has developed an action plan to counter illegal logging, known as the Forest Law Enforcement, Governance and Trade (FLEGT) action plan (see also Overdevest & Zeitlin 2014). This action plan, approved in Council Conclusions in October 2003, requires the EU to develop Voluntary Partnership Agreements with producer countries on timber licensing. Producer countries entering into such agreements with the EU commit to exporting only legally logged timber to the EU (European Commission 2003). Other key elements in the FLEGT action plan include the development of member-state procurement policies to purchase timber from legal sources; the promotion of private-sector initiatives, including codes of conduct and independent monitoring; and the exercise of due diligence by export credit agencies and ﬁnancial institutions that fund logging in producer countries (Euro- pean Commission 2003). In 2008, the European Commission proposed a new, legally binding EU Timber Regulation that recognizes certiﬁcation as a tool in the risk-assessment procedure when timber is imported into the EU. This timber regulation, adopted by the European Parlia- ment and the Council in 2010, will enter into force in 2013. According to the EU’s policy on green procurement, legality should be a minimum require- ment for wood-based products (European Commission 2008), but this policy is not legally binding on the member states. Unlike trade, public procurement is a member-state competence, which leaves the ﬁnal decision to each individual member state. Six EU member states – Belgium,3 Denmark,4 France, Germany,5 the Netherlands,6 and the UK7 – have adopted procure- ment policies on purchasing timber from legal and sustainable sources. Outside the EU, such policies have been adopted by Japan,8 New Zealand, and Norway.9 Several other countries, mostly EU member states, are considering developing timber procurement policies. Although the US in 2008 became the ﬁrst state to legislate against the handling of timber which is illegal according to the laws of the country the timber originated from (see Cashore & Stone 2014), it has thus far not been willing to develop procurement policies for timber.
